focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Saint's Basin showed itself a few yards at a time through the lantern in August's hand, which had smoked its own glass yellow. He had left the motorcar on the section road because the field track was not a road anymore but two chopped ruts and a soft shoulder where the diggers' wagon had cut in after the last little rain. The moon stayed snagged behind a low wool of dust and cloud. Each step off the road tugged at his shoes. Mud caught him by the heel and then let go with a wet sucking sound that traveled too far in the open dark.

Knapp's note kept crackling in his coat pocket whenever he bent. Final papers tomorrow. Need full auction schedule, title corrections, deficiency estimates. The words had been typed hard enough to emboss the back of the sheet. August could feel their ridges through the wool, a set of hard fingertips already at the center of his chest.

The basin lay quartered open where Hester Blum and her little congregation had cut it. Even in the dark he could make out the spoil ridge, the slumped edge where the wall had given way on Dell Vale, the blacker stripe of damp earth showing through powder-gray crust. Someone had left a broken shovel handle propped against a stake. A twist of bandage, dirty now and stiff as paper, had snagged on a clod near the trench lip and fluttered every time the wind found it. That scrap bothered him more than the hole did. A boy's broken arm could be entered in a report as injury at unauthorized excavation. A strip of linen fouled with clay made the thing vulgar.

He set the lantern down and listened.

The fields south of the Vale place whispered the way dry wheat always did at night, a sound like hands worrying cheap cloth. Somewhere farther off a sheet of tin knocked once, then not again. The seep itself mad...
prev_tail_excerpt:
"Keep your voice down," she said. "He just got quiet."

"I won't wake him." Thea heard how careful that sounded and hated it. "Can you come out a minute?"

Cora's hand tightened on the wet cloth. "Is it August?"

"No."

"Then what?"

Thea glanced toward the front room. Through the thin wall came a small dry click of metal against metal, Dell refusing rest even one-handed. "Not where he can hear."

Cora did not move. "Don't pad it for me. If it's bad, say bad."

"I found something in the schoolhouse," Thea said. "Ezra hid it there. I think he meant you to have it."

The muslin sagged in Cora's hand and dripped onto the threshold. "What sort of thing?"

"A letter. And Otto's basin marks copied out."

Cora's mouth went flat. "What does he say?"

Thea had meant to give her one careful sentence, something that would get them clear of Dell before the words did their work. Instead the bluntest part came first. "He says August lied later. He also says the first yes was Ezra's."

Nothing moved in the kitchen now but the kettle giving its little tin complaint. Cora stared at her as if the words had arrived in the wrong language and might, if she held still enough, change back. When she spoke, her voice had gone low enough to cut.

"You're sure."

"His hand. His shame all over it. I didn't bring it here to invent a cruelty."

Cora wiped the wet muslin once across her palm though there was nothing on it to clean. "Dell doesn't hear a word before I do."

"No."

"Good." She set the cloth on the sill, stepped through the door, and pulled it nearly shut behind her. "Give it to me outside."
